region: Larkmont and the Larkmont Valley (demo area)
note: A general demo field guide — common, cosmopolitan wild species. The data does not document any specific real geographic area and does not include protected, rare, or endangered species.

species.peak: Main bloom in spring, but flowers almost year-round
id_marks: A single yellow flower on a hollow stem
Toothed leaves in a basal rosette
A white fluff-ball of wind-borne seeds
species.info: A cosmopolitan perennial herb common in almost every temperate region of the world; one of the most familiar plants on lawns and in gardens.
species.habitat: Lawns, gardens, roadsides, and disturbed ground
species.blurb_he: The dandelion is everyone's favorite 'weed': a bright yellow flower that opens with the sun and closes in the evening, and after pollination turns into a perfect fluff-ball of seeds. Every child knows the ritual of blowing on the ball and scattering the seeds in the wind.
species.where_when_he: Found on almost every lawn, garden, and patch of disturbed ground. The main bloom is in spring, but under favorable conditions flowers can be seen nearly year-round.
species.folklore_he: In many traditions people counted 'how many puffs' it takes to scatter all the fluff — as a game for guessing the hour or making wishes. The young leaves are eaten in salads, and the root was used as a coffee substitute.
species.fun_fact_he: Each seed carries a tiny 'parachute' of fibers, and when the air's humidity rises the parachute closes — a mechanism that keeps the seed from taking off on a rainy day, when it wouldn't disperse far.

species.peak: Blooms from spring to late summer
id_marks: A three-part leaf (three leaflets) with a pale marking
A round white flower head
Creeping stems that take root
species.info: A creeping perennial legume, common on lawns throughout the temperate world; it fixes nitrogen and is an important food source for bees.
species.habitat: Lawns, meadows, and path edges
species.blurb_he: White clover is a low carpet of three-part leaves and white flower heads that attract bees. It 'improves' the soil by fixing nitrogen from the air, which is exactly why farmers and lawn growers actually like it.
species.where_when_he: On almost every lawn and meadow. It blooms from spring through summer, and the white flower heads stand out above the green grass.
species.folklore_he: A clover leaf with four leaflets (instead of three) is regarded in Western culture as a rare symbol of luck — a mutation that appears in roughly one of every several thousand leaves.
species.fun_fact_he: A single flower head is made up of dozens of tiny flowers, each offering a drop of nectar — so a clover field can feed an entire bee colony.

species.peak: Peak bloom in spring
id_marks: A small 'flower' with white petals and a yellow center
Spoon-shaped leaves in a low rosette
Closes at night and opens with daylight
species.info: A dwarf perennial lawn plant, common in every temperate region; it blooms again and again even after mowing.
species.habitat: Mown lawns, gardens, and parks
species.blurb_he: The daisy is the small, stubborn flower of the lawn: mowing after mowing, it keeps coming back and blooming. Its English name 'daisy' derives from 'day's eye' — because it opens in the morning and closes at night.
species.where_when_he: Mown lawns, parks, and gardens. The densest bloom is in spring, but individual flowers can be seen for most of the year.
species.folklore_he: A daisy chain — weaving the stems together — is a classic children's game, and also the source of the 'loves me / loves me not' game of plucking the petals.
species.fun_fact_he: What looks like a single 'flower' is actually an inflorescence of hundreds of tiny florets: the white ones around the rim and the yellow ones in the center.

species.peak: Red bloom at the height of spring
id_marks: Four red petals, thin as silk
Sometimes a dark spot at the base of the petal
A seed capsule shaped like a saltshaker
species.info: A common annual of fields and roadsides in the temperate world; it blooms in a red flood after the winter rains.
species.habitat: Fields, roadsides, and disturbed ground
species.blurb_he: The red poppy is spring's flash of color: petals as thin as silk paper that shimmer in the wind. It is an annual that germinates masses of seeds after the rain, painting whole fields red for a few weeks.
species.where_when_he: Fields, roadsides, and disturbed ground. The peak is in spring; after a week or two the petals fall and the flower turns into a seed capsule.
species.folklore_he: The red poppy became a symbol of remembrance in the world wars, and in many places people wear it as a cloth flower in memory of the fallen.
species.fun_fact_he: The seed capsule is perforated with tiny holes beneath its 'cap,' and when the wind shakes the stem the seeds scatter out as if from a saltshaker.

id_marks: Long, narrow leaves with prominent parallel veins
A short flower spike on a grooved stem
A whitish ring of stamens around the spike
species.info: A cosmopolitan perennial herb of path edges and lawns; very resistant to trampling.
species.habitat: Lawns, path edges, and trodden ground
species.blurb_he: The plantain is the classic 'path-edge' plant — it actually thrives where it gets trodden on. Its narrow, parallel-veined leaves and the small spike with its ring of stamens are familiar from every garden and sidewalk.
species.where_when_he: Path edges, lawns, and trodden ground. Active from spring through summer.
species.folklore_he: In folk tradition people crushed a plantain leaf and placed it on stings and scratches as an improvised 'field dressing.'
species.fun_fact_he: The flexible stem served generations of children for a 'flower-shooting' game — looping it around the head and firing it off like a sling.

species.peak: Sprouts in spring–summer, produces plumes in autumn
id_marks: A hollow, tall stem (up to several meters)
A feathery, purplish-silver flower plume
Long, flat leaves arranged in two rows
species.info: One of the most widespread flowering plants in the world; it forms dense reed thickets around fresh water.
species.habitat: Lake shores, ponds, and water channels
species.blurb_he: The common reed is one of the most widespread plants in the entire world — a tall, whispering thicket around every lake and water channel. Its feathery plumes sway in the wind, and many water birds nest among them.
species.where_when_he: Lake shores, ponds, marshes, and channels. Green in summer; in autumn and winter the stems yellow and the plumes turn silvery.
species.folklore_he: Reeds have been used since ancient times for roofs, mats, and simple wind instruments, and the sound of the wind in the reed thicket appears in poetry and music.
species.fun_fact_he: A single reed segment can send underground runners for tens of meters, so an entire 'reed forest' is sometimes one giant clone.

id_marks: Male with a gray 'cap' and a black chin
Female a uniform sandy brown
A thick, short seed-eater's bill
species.info: One of the most common birds in the world, accompanying humans in almost every settlement; it eats seeds and crumbs.
species.habitat: Cities, gardens, and around human buildings
species.blurb_he: The house sparrow is a long-standing urban neighbor: chirping flocks that live around houses, gardens, and cafés, gleaning crumbs from under the tables. It is one of the birds that spread in humanity's wake to almost every corner of every settlement.
species.where_when_he: Year-round around buildings, gardens, and built-up areas. It nests in holes and gutters and raises several broods of chicks per season.
species.folklore_he: The sparrow appears in proverbs and poetry as a symbol of the small, everyday thing that is precious precisely because of it — the everyday 'free bird' (in Hebrew, 'dror' means both sparrow and freedom).
species.fun_fact_he: Sparrows love a 'dust bath': they roll in dry soil to clean parasites from their feathers — a familiar sight in any flower bed.

id_marks: A gray body with two dark bars on the wing
A green-purple sheen on the neck
Many color variations among city individuals
species.info: The ancestor of all domestic pigeons; the urban form is common in every city in the world.
species.habitat: Cities, squares, bridges, and buildings
species.blurb_he: The rock dove is the classic city pigeon — the one that struts in the squares and nests on window ledges and bridges. From this wild form all breeds of domestic pigeons were tamed, including the famous homing pigeons.
species.where_when_he: Year-round in any built environment, from the city square to farm buildings. It nests on concrete ledges and beams and raises chicks in almost every month.
species.folklore_he: The dove as a symbol of peace and of carrying messages has accompanied culture for thousands of years; homing pigeons carried messages in battles and important missions.
species.fun_fact_he: Pigeons also navigate by the Earth's magnetic field, which is how a homing pigeon can find its way home from hundreds of kilometers away.

id_marks: A glossy black male with an orange bill and eye-ring
A dark brown female
A rich, flute-like song from a treetop or roof
species.info: A common songbird of gardens and parks; known for its beautiful song in the morning and evening.
species.habitat: Gardens, parks, and groves
species.blurb_he: The blackbird is the garden's singer: a coal-black male with a bright orange bill that sings long, flute-like melodies from a rooftop or tree, mainly at dawn and dusk. It digs through the leaf litter in search of worms.
species.where_when_he: Year-round in gardens, parks, and groves. The song intensifies greatly in spring, during the nesting and territory-marking season.
species.folklore_he: The blackbird's voice is considered one of the most beautiful songs in the bird world, and it appears in poetry and music as a symbol of the morning song.
species.fun_fact_he: Each male improvises its own variations on the tune, and city blackbirds sometimes even 'quote' phone tones or alarm sounds they have heard.

species.peak: Present in spring and summer, during the nesting season
id_marks: A metallic-blue back and a pale underside
A rusty-red forehead and throat
A long forked tail with two 'streamers'
species.info: A migratory bird that nests in summer; it flies fast over fields and water hunting flying insects.
species.habitat: Open areas, cowsheds, and farm buildings
species.blurb_he: The swallow is the fighter pilot of the open skies: with its long forked tail it cuts sharp turns over fields and water, catching insects on the wing. It builds a neat mud nest on beams and under canopies.
species.where_when_he: It arrives in spring, nests through the summer, and sets out in autumn on a long migration. It is seen gliding over open areas and water sources.
species.folklore_he: The return of the swallows in spring has always been seen as a herald of the changing season, hence the saying 'one swallow does not make a spring.'
species.fun_fact_he: A pair of swallows often returns to the very same nest year after year, after a migration of thousands of kilometers each way.

id_marks: Black feathers with a purple-green sheen and pale spots
A yellow bill in the breeding season
Flies in dense, coordinated flocks
species.info: A highly social bird that forms huge flocks; a talented vocal mimic. Common around the world.
species.habitat: Fields, lawns, and around settlements
species.blurb_he: The starling is famous for its 'murmuration' — huge flocks of thousands of birds that twist through the sky like a living, shifting cloud. Up close it is a spotted, glossy bird, and an excellent vocal mimic that weaves into its song sounds it has gathered from its surroundings.
species.where_when_he: Year-round in fields, lawns, and around settlements; the large flocks and the aerial displays are especially striking before sunset in the cold season.
species.folklore_he: The starling's mimicry has occupied writers and composers; some kept starlings that learned to imitate melodies and words.
species.fun_fact_he: In a murmuration each bird coordinates its movement with only about its seven nearest neighbors — and so the coordinated 'flow' of the whole flock arises without a 'conductor.'

id_marks: A male with a metallic-green head and a white neck ring
A mottled brown female
A blue-purple speculum on the wing
species.info: The most widespread wild duck in the world and the ancestor of most domestic ducks; common on every body of fresh water.
species.habitat: Lakes, ponds, and slow streams
species.blurb_he: The mallard is the classic wild duck of the lake and pond: a male with a glossy green head and white ring, and a brown, camouflaged female. The pairs swim together, 'stand on their heads' to gather food from the bottom, and raise a charming string of ducklings.
species.where_when_he: Year-round on every body of fresh water — a lake, a park pond, or a slow stream. The ducklings are most conspicuous in spring and early summer.
species.folklore_he: The duck familiar from children's books and nursery songs is usually based on the mallard — the 'quack' of the park pond.
species.fun_fact_he: Mallard ducklings 'imprint' on the first figure they see upon hatching and follow it — usually the mother, but sometimes any other moving creature.

id_marks: A white, heart-shaped face
A white underside and a golden, mottled back
Completely silent nocturnal flight
species.info: A cosmopolitan nocturnal owl found on almost every continent; it hunts rodents in open areas. It aids agriculture as 'biological pest control.'
species.habitat: Open areas, farm buildings, and barns
species.blurb_he: The barn owl is the white ghost of the night: its white, heart-shaped face and utterly silent flight make it a perfect rodent hunter. It is one of the most widespread birds in the world and is found on almost every continent.
species.where_when_he: Year-round, active mainly at night over fields and open areas. It nests in attics, barns, and dedicated nest boxes.
species.folklore_he: Its white face and harsh nighttime call wrapped the barn owl in an aura of mystery in many cultures, even though it is actually the farmer's friend.
species.fun_fact_he: Many farmers put up nest boxes for barn owls as 'biological pest control': a single barn owl family preys on thousands of rodents in a season, saving on spraying.

species.peak: Seen mainly during the migration seasons near water
id_marks: A dark brown back and a strikingly white underside
A dark eye-stripe across the cheek
Dives feet-first into the water to catch fish
species.info: A cosmopolitan fish-eating raptor found on almost every continent; it feeds almost exclusively on fish it catches from the water.
species.habitat: Lake shores, reservoirs, and rivers
species.blurb_he: The osprey is the fish-eagle: a large, nearly cosmopolitan raptor that specializes exclusively in catching fish. It hovers over the water, spots a fish, then dives feet-first and snatches it in its talons with an impressive splash.
species.where_when_he: Seen mainly during the migration seasons (spring and autumn) over lakes, reservoirs, and rivers on its way between its nesting and wintering grounds.
species.folklore_he: The osprey's spectacular fishing dive has made it a favorite symbol among anglers and birdwatchers all over the world.
species.fun_fact_he: The osprey's foot has an outer toe that rotates forward and backward and spiny pads — adaptations that let it grip a slippery fish without it slipping away.

species.peak: Common from spring to autumn
id_marks: Orange-pink wings with black and white at the tips
Small eyespots along the edge of the hindwing
A fast, migratory flier
species.info: One of the most widespread butterflies in the world — found on almost every continent and performing long-distance migrations.
species.habitat: Flower fields, gardens, and open areas
species.blurb_he: The painted lady is probably the most widespread butterfly in the world: it is found on almost every continent and performs long seasonal migrations, sometimes thousands of kilometers, over successive generations. Its orange-pink wings are mottled with black and white.
species.where_when_he: Gardens, flower fields, and open areas, mainly from spring to autumn. In good years there are 'migration years' with hundreds of individuals at once.
species.folklore_he: Because of its worldwide distribution and migrations, it is regarded as the classic symbol of the 'wandering' butterfly — a butterfly that belongs to no single place.
species.fun_fact_he: No single butterfly completes the whole migration: the journey takes place over several generations, and the offspring continue the route their parents began.

species.peak: Common from spring to autumn
id_marks: White wings with gray-black tips
One dark spot (male) or two (female) on the forewing
A velvety green caterpillar on brassica leaves
species.info: A very common white butterfly that spread with agriculture to almost the whole world; the caterpillar loves cabbage and garden plants.
species.habitat: Vegetable gardens, fields, and beds
species.blurb_he: The cabbage white is the white butterfly everyone knows from the garden: it flutters lightly between the beds and lays eggs on the leaves of cabbage, radish, and other garden plants. It spread in the wake of agriculture and is now common across almost the whole world.
species.where_when_he: Vegetable gardens, fields, and beds, from spring to autumn — sometimes several generations in the same year.
species.folklore_he: Its green caterpillar is known to every gardener as the 'cabbage eater,' and the gardeners' struggle against it is a fixed part of vegetable-garden culture.
species.fun_fact_he: The caterpillar is excellent at camouflage: velvety green exactly the color of the leaf, with a fine pale stripe that mimics the leaf's central vein.

species.peak: Active from spring to autumn
id_marks: A red dome with seven black spots
A black head with two white patches
A grayish, spotted larva like a tiny 'crocodile'
species.info: A familiar lucky beetle; it preys on aphids and is therefore considered the gardener's friend.
species.habitat: Gardens, fields, and herbaceous vegetation
species.blurb_he: The seven-spot ladybird is the classic ladybug — a red dome with seven black spots. Both the adult and its larva prey on aphids in great numbers, so gardeners and farmers welcome it gladly.
species.where_when_he: Gardens, fields, and green vegetation, from spring to autumn; in winter it sometimes gathers in groups in sheltered spots.
species.folklore_he: In many languages its name is linked to blessing and luck ('beetle of Our Lady'), and in children's games people place it on a finger and ask it to fly off for a 'wish.'
species.fun_fact_he: When attacked it secretes a drop of bitter yellow fluid from its leg joints ('reflex bleeding') that deters predators — hence also its warning red color.

species.peak: Active as long as there is bloom
id_marks: A golden-brown body with dark stripes and hairiness
'Pollen baskets' on the hind legs
Lives in a large social colony
species.info: The most important pollinator in the world; it lives in social colonies and produces honey. Critical for pollinating agricultural crops.
species.habitat: Anywhere with flowers — gardens, fields, and groves
species.blurb_he: The honey bee is one of the most important pollinators in the world: a single colony numbers tens of thousands of workers that gather nectar and pollen, communicate through dance, and produce honey. Without them a large share of our fruits and vegetables simply would not set.
species.where_when_he: Anywhere there is bloom — gardens, fields, parks, and groves — for most of the year, as long as there are open flowers.
species.folklore_he: Honey and the hive have accompanied culture for thousands of years as a symbol of diligence, sweetness, and teamwork; 'busy as a bee' is an old compliment.
species.fun_fact_he: A worker that has found a rich nectar source performs a 'waggle dance' in the hive that encodes, in its angle and duration, the direction and distance to the flowers.

species.peak: Common in summer and autumn near water
id_marks: A yellow-orange body
Broad hindwings adapted for gliding
Hovers for long stretches almost without landing
species.info: Probably the most widespread dragonfly in the world; an excellent glider that makes cross-continental migrations.
species.habitat: Ponds, puddles, and temporary bodies of water
species.blurb_he: The wandering glider is the champion of gliding: its broad hindwings let it hover for long hours and cover enormous distances. It is probably the most widespread dragonfly in the world and makes seasonal migrations between continents.
species.where_when_he: Near ponds, puddles, and temporary bodies of water, mainly in summer and autumn. It is usually seen hovering in a swarm over open ground.
species.folklore_he: The sudden appearance of a swarm of dragonflies after rain has been linked in many cultures to the coming of water and abundance.
species.fun_fact_he: Its larvae develop at record speed in temporary rain puddles — a race against time to complete metamorphosis before the water evaporates.

species.peak: Active mainly on warm evenings and nights
id_marks: Smooth, grayish, semi-translucent skin
Adhesive pads under the toes
Active at night near light sources
species.info: A small, nocturnal house lizard, common in warm regions all over the world; it feeds on insects near lights.
species.habitat: House walls, ceilings, and around outdoor lighting
species.blurb_he: The house gecko is the nocturnal wall-neighbor: a small, translucent-gray lizard that climbs walls and ceilings with ease thanks to special adhesive pads on its toes, lying in wait for insects around a yard lamp. It is common in warm regions all over the world.
species.where_when_he: Year-round on house walls, ceilings, and surfaces around outdoor lighting; active mainly after dark.
species.folklore_he: In many cultures a gecko in the house is considered a good sign and a natural house-guard — after all, it eats mosquitoes and flies.
species.fun_fact_he: When a predator grabs it by the tail, the gecko 'sheds' the tail — it keeps wriggling and distracts the predator while the gecko escapes, and after a while a new tail grows back.

id_marks: A small, smooth, worm-like body
Tiny, vestigial eyes beneath the scales
A uniform purplish-brown color
species.info: A tiny, non-venomous snake that lives in the soil and litter; one of the most widespread snakes in the world, spreading with potting soil.
species.habitat: Garden soil, under stones, and in flowerpots
species.blurb_he: The brahminy blind snake looks more like a shiny earthworm than a snake: it is tiny, completely harmless, and lives under stones and in garden soil. Thanks to its size and habits it has spread with potting soil to almost the whole world.
species.where_when_he: Year-round in moist soil, under stones, in leaf piles, and in flowerpots; almost always below the surface.
species.folklore_he: It is very easy to mistake for a worm, and so it 'travels' between gardens inside flowerpots without anyone noticing.
species.fun_fact_he: All individuals are females that reproduce without fertilization (parthenogenesis) — so a single individual arriving in potting soil can found a new population on its own.

species.peak: Pops up in the moist autumn, mainly under trees
id_marks: A bright red cap with white flecks
A white ring on the stem
A swollen base with remnants of an 'egg'
species.info: The 'fairy-tale' mushroom with the red-and-white cap; poisonous. It forms root partnerships (mycorrhiza) with trees.
species.habitat: Moist groves, under trees
species.blurb_he: The fly agaric is the 'fairy-tale mushroom' with the bright red cap and white dots — the one pictured in every children's book and nursery decoration. Despite its beauty it is poisonous, and must not be eaten.
species.where_when_he: It emerges in the moist autumn, mainly in groves and forests under trees, with which it lives in a root-level symbiosis.
species.folklore_he: The red-and-white cap has become the icon of a 'mushroom' in popular culture — from video games to garden ornaments.
species.fun_fact_he: The white dots are not a permanent part of the cap but remnants of the 'veil' that wrapped the young mushroom — and heavy rain can wash them away.

species.peak: Appears after the autumn rains on lawns
id_marks: A round, smooth white cap
Pink gills that darken with age
A pleasant mushroomy smell
species.info: A wild relative of the cultivated mushroom (button mushroom); it appears on lawns after rain.
species.habitat: Lawns, meadows, and grazing fields
species.blurb_he: The field mushroom is the wild cousin of the 'button mushroom' from the supermarket: a round white cap that pops up in groups on the lawn after rain, with pink gills that darken as it matures.
species.where_when_he: It appears after the autumn rains on lawns, in meadows, and in grazing fields, sometimes in round 'fairy rings.'
species.folklore_he: Groups of mushrooms that come up in a circle are called 'fairy rings' in folklore, and in the past they were attributed magical power.
species.fun_fact_he: The 'ring' forms because the fungus grows underground outward in an expanding circle, producing fruiting bodies exactly at the growing edge.

species.peak: Emerges in autumn along path edges and lawns
id_marks: A tall white cap covered in shaggy scales
Dissolves into black ink from the edge upward
Grows fast and is short-lived
species.info: A tall white mushroom that breaks down into a black, ink-like liquid within a day or two.
species.habitat: Lawns, roadsides, and disturbed ground
species.blurb_he: The ink cap starts as a tall, scaly white cap, and within a day or two 'melts' from the edge of the cap upward into a black, ink-like liquid. Its fast, brief appearance makes any encounter with it a matter of timing.
species.where_when_he: It emerges in autumn along paths, on lawns, and in disturbed ground — sometimes in a group that springs up overnight and vanishes within two days.
species.folklore_he: The black liquid was once actually used as writing ink, hence its name.
species.fun_fact_he: The 'melting' is not random decay but a means of dispersal: the cap dissolves itself into liquid to release its spores and let them be washed away and scattered.

species.peak: Usually seen at dusk and at night
id_marks: Reddish-brown fur and a pale underside
A thick, bushy tail with a white tip
Erect ears and a pointed snout
species.info: The most common and widespread carnivore in the world; it lives in almost every habitat, including the edges of cities. It feeds on rodents, insects, and fruit.
species.habitat: Fields, groves, parks, and the edges of settlements
species.blurb_he: The red fox is the most widely distributed carnivore in the world: from field and grove to the edges of cities. Its reddish fur, its bushy white-tipped tail, and its proverbial cleverness have made it a central figure in fables and tales.
species.where_when_he: Year-round, active mainly at dusk and at night, in fields, groves, parks, and the edges of settlements. A flexible omnivore — rodents, insects, fruit, and scraps.
species.folklore_he: In dozens of cultures the fox is the symbol of cunning and sophistication — from Aesop's 'The Fox and the Grapes' to cunning foxes in folk tales.
species.fun_fact_he: Its hearing is so sharp that it locates a rodent under snow or soil by sound alone, then leaps in a vertical 'mouse-dive' to land precisely on it.

species.peak: Seen mainly in the early morning and at dusk
id_marks: A compact grayish-brown body
Medium ears (shorter than a hare's)
A short tail with a white underside
species.info: The ancestor of domestic rabbits; it lives in burrow-digging colonies. It was introduced by humans to many regions of the world.
species.habitat: Fields, lawns, and open areas with burrows
species.blurb_he: The European rabbit is the wild ancestor of all domestic rabbits. It lives in social colonies that dig an extensive system of burrows, feeds on grass, and bolts for the burrow in zigzag hops at a moment of danger. Humans have spread it to many regions of the world.
species.where_when_he: Fields, lawns, and open areas, usually near burrow entrances; active mainly during the cool hours of morning and evening.
species.folklore_he: Rabbits symbolize fertility and spring in many cultures, and the figure of the rabbit appears from folk tales to the 'Easter bunny.'
species.fun_fact_he: A rabbit colony digs an entire underground 'city' (a warren) with rooms, corridors, and multiple escape exits, passed down through generations.

id_marks: A small grayish-brown body and a long, bare tail
Relatively large ears
Active mainly at night
species.info: One of the most common mammals in the world; it accompanies humans everywhere. Also the most studied laboratory animal.
species.habitat: Houses, warehouses, fields, and around humans
species.blurb_he: The house mouse is humanity's tiny companion everywhere in the world: quick, curious, and adaptable, living in walls, warehouses, and fields. It is also the most studied mammal in science, and has contributed greatly to our understanding of biology and medicine.
species.where_when_he: Year-round, active mainly at night, in houses, warehouses, farm buildings, and nearby fields.
species.folklore_he: The small, clever mouse stars in countless tales, cartoons, and proverbs — usually as a weak but smart character that outwits someone stronger.
species.fun_fact_he: Mice 'sing' courtship songs at ultrasonic frequencies that the human ear cannot detect — whole melodies that we discover only with instruments.

species.peak: Active by day, mainly in the morning and afternoon
id_marks: Grayish fur and a well-groomed, glossy tail
Rounded ears
Nimble climbing and leaping among branches
species.info: A diurnal climbing rodent common in parks and groves; it was introduced to various regions of the world and acclimatized successfully.
species.habitat: Parks, groves, and ornamental trees
species.blurb_he: The grey squirrel is the star of the park: a nimble, diurnal rodent that climbs, leaps between branches, and spends the day gathering nuts. In urban parks it sometimes grows so used to people that it comes up to beg for a treat.
species.where_when_he: Year-round, by day, in parks, groves, and tree-lined avenues. In autumn it is especially busy storing food for the winter.
species.folklore_he: The image of the squirrel hoarding nuts for the winter has become a symbol of thrift and planning ahead in children's stories and proverbs.
species.fun_fact_he: The squirrel buries thousands of nuts in autumn and forgets some of the caches — and so it inadvertently 'plants' new trees and helps the forest renew itself.

species.peak: Active on summer evenings after sunset
id_marks: A tiny bat (about 5 grams)
Brown fur and a dark face
Fluttering, agile flight after sunset
species.info: One of the small, common insect-eating bats; it hunts mosquitoes and flying insects at night. The gardener's friend.
species.habitat: Gardens, parks, and around water, near buildings
species.blurb_he: The pipistrelle is a tiny insect-eating bat — so small it fits in the palm of a hand — that appears at nightfall and flutters over gardens and water hunting mosquitoes. It 'sees' in the dark using echolocation: sound waves that bounce back and map its surroundings.
species.where_when_he: Active at night from spring to autumn over gardens, parks, and around water; in the cold winter it goes into hibernation.
species.folklore_he: In folklore bats acquired a mysterious aura as creatures of the night, even though most of them — like the pipistrelle — are nothing but small, harmless mosquito-hunters.
species.fun_fact_he: A single pipistrelle preys on thousands of mosquitoes and small insects in one night — a free, natural 'pest-control service' for any garden.
